prompt: Why is delivering an AI feature usually run as a time-boxed experiment rather than a fixed-scope build with a binary 'done'?
choices: Because AI features are cheaper to build than normal features, so planning is unnecessary
Because the output is probabilistic and you don't know up front whether the model is good enough on your real data, so the project is discovery-shaped
Because experiments never have deadlines or budgets
Because regulations forbid giving AI features a delivery date
explanation: An AI feature is probabilistic and there's rarely one correct output, and the key unknown — is the model good enough on your data — is untested at the start. So you run a discovery-shaped experiment with a success metric and a kill option, not a fixed-scope build you sign off as binary done.

prompt: A feasibility spike on real data shows your AI feature can't reach the agreed quality threshold, even after tuning. From a delivery standpoint, what is the right framing of a decision to stop the pilot?
choices: A failure that should be hidden from stakeholders to protect the team
A success: a clear 'no' that frees budget and people for a better bet
Irrelevant, because a funded pilot must always ship something
Proof that AI can never work for this organisation
explanation: Killing a pilot that can't clear the bar is a successful outcome of the experiment — it returns budget and people to better bets. The real failure is the sunk-cost trap: pouring more money into a weak pilot just because it's already funded.

prompt: Which of the following are AI-specific risks that belong on an AI project's risk register? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Data isn't available, clean, or labelled enough to evaluate quality
Per-call cost is fine in a demo but breaks the business case at real volume
A privacy or regulatory gate must clear before the feature can ship
The office Wi-Fi is occasionally slow in the afternoon
explanation: Data readiness, cost-at-scale, and a compliance gate are real AI-specific delivery risks with named owners and mitigations. Generic office annoyances aren't project risks for this feature and don't belong on the register.

prompt: How should acceptance criteria (the definition of 'done') be written for a non-deterministic AI feature?
choices: As an exact expected output string the feature must return every time
As measurable properties: eval scores over a threshold on a held-out set, guardrails passing, and cost/latency within budget
As 'it looked good in the demo to the stakeholders'
As the number of prompts the team ran during development
explanation: Because there's no single correct output to assert, acceptance is statistical: a quality score over a threshold on held-out data, guardrail pass rate, and cost/latency budgets. A demo impression or a prompt count tells you nothing about whether it's actually good enough to ship.

prompt: TRADE-OFF: At the start of a pilot you can either invest two weeks building a thorough, stratified golden eval set, or ship the pilot faster with a thin, happy-path eval set. What is the trade-off?
choices: There is no trade-off; the thin eval set is always better because it is faster
A thorough eval set delays the pilot but measures quality you can trust (including hard cases); a thin one starts faster but can report a misleadingly high score and let a weak feature pass the gate
A thorough eval set is always required and a thin one can never be used under any circumstances
Both eval sets give identical results, so the choice only affects documentation
explanation: It's a real trade-off between speed and trustworthy measurement. A thin happy-path eval set gets you to a number faster but can hide failures on hard cases and pass a weak feature; a stratified set costs time but the score actually means something. The right call depends on the stakes — a low-risk internal tool can start thin, a customer-facing or regulated feature can't.
